<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9002

**Category:** Typeclass Resolution · **Severity:** Error

## Message

```
@impl/@instance must annotate a const declaration
```

## Explanation

@impl (or @instance) is used to register a typeclass instance.

Preferred JSDoc syntax:
  /** @impl Show<Point> */
  const showPoint: Show<Point> = { show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})` };

Legacy decorator syntax:
  @impl("Show<Point>")
  const showPoint: Show<Point> = { show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})` };

Incorrect:
  @impl  // ✗ Not a const
  function showPoint() { ... }

  @impl  // ✗ Missing type annotation
  const showPoint = { ... };

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Show<Point> */
// error: TS9002 — @impl/@instance must annotate a const declaration
function showPoint(p: Point): string {
  return `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`;
}
```

## How to fix

An instance is a **value** that implements the typeclass interface, so `@impl` must sit on a `const` declaration with an explicit `Typeclass<Type>` annotation:

```typescript
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Show<Point> */
const showPoint: Show<Point> = {
  show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};

// Access via the companion: Point.Show.show(p)
```

The legacy decorator form `@impl("Show<Point>")` on the same `const` also works, but the JSDoc form is portable — it type-checks cleanly under plain `tsc`. If you had a helper function, move its body into the instance object's method rather than annotating the function itself.
